description: A 1.5-hp split air conditioner with all 33 parts hand-modeled from three.js primitives — zero external models or textures, every texture painted on Canvas. Watch it assemble part by part, blow the whole machine into a labeled exploded view, then power it on: the cross-flow fan spins, louvers sweep, cold air sinks and pools across the floor — and heat mode reverses the cycle, courtesy of the four-way valve.
title: Arctic Breeze — AC Teardown

description: A 65-inch 4K OLED TV in a full living-room scene, 21 parts hand-modeled from three.js primitives with Canvas-painted textures. The panel stack builds layer by layer — panel, flex cables, T-CON, graphite film, boards — and explodes toward the viewer like a mille-feuille. In watch mode the screen genuinely lights the room via RectAreaLight: aurora reel, test bars, or a synthwave racing game with signal pulses running down the HDMI cable.
title: Polar Night — OLED TV

description: A fictional white game console — an homage to a famous teardown video — standing on a gamer-room desk. 20 parts from motherboard, liquid metal and heat spreader to dust catchers and white side panels. One-click exploded view floats the internals above the desk; game mode lights the monitor, breathes the LED strips, and ties fan RPM to live SoC temperature and noise readouts.
title: White Night V — Console Teardown

description: A football pitch stacked with 1,095 giant dominoes along a 1.1 km three-dimensional course: snake runs, a forked spiral, an 11-step staircase, a 4.6 m skybridge, a diving relay, a 15×11 V-wave array, and a finale that knocks a giant football into the goal. Every fall is real rigid-body physics — Rapier WASM, one InstancedMesh, a single draw call — with no scripted animation anywhere.
title: Domino Pitch — 1,095 Falling Dominoes
